urine test. 212 For Americans, by contrast, the right to privacy is, at its metaphoric core, a

right to hide behind the walls of one’s own home. Those who have abandoned the

protection of the home, and a fortiori the protection of clothing, have at best a diminished

claim to privacy.

VII

Indeed, continental ideas of privacy are just not much at home in American legal

culture. To be sure, there is certainly American law on the books that sounds something

like what we find on the books of Germany or France. American law has its famous four

forms of the privacy tort, as analyzed by Prosser in 1960: intrusion upon seclusion213 ;

    Not every court takes this attitude. Cf. Michaels v. Internet Entertain ment Group, Inc., 5 F. Supp 2d 823
(C.D. Cal. 1998).
212
    For applications of this rule, see Soehring, Presserecht, 450-452.

68
 appropriation of the name or likeness of another 214 ; public disclosure of private facts

“not of legitimate concern to the public”215 ; and disclosure of private facts in such a way

as to portray victims in a “false light.”216 There is considerable legislation too, like the

Video Privacy Protection Act of 1987, passed in reaction to journalistic investigations of

Robert Bork, and various other acts and bills, both state and federal. American

legislatures do pass privacy protection statutes of various kinds217 —especially, as Jeffrey

Rosen has observed, in the wake of “heartstring- tugging” scandals. 218 While many of

these statutes treat the government as the principal threat to privacy, not all of them do.

Though less than a third of the states have general laws on the protection of privacy, there

certainly are state protections. 219 There are even state constitutional protections for

privacy. 220 There are certainly decisions giving protection to one’s image, notably cases

involving the nude or sexually charged images of young women who did not

intentionally pose in a provocative way, 221 or who are the victims of sexual assaults, 222 or

who otherwise seem to be walking “the path of rectitude.”223 There are certainly cases

involving non- governmental invasions of the “privacy of the home,”224 and especially of
